name: Dullahan
description: The Dullahan, also known as the Headless Horseman, is a fearsome figure in Irish folklore. This spectral rider is a harbinger of death, said to appear before someone is about to die. The Dullahan rides a black horse through the countryside, carrying his own head, which glows eerily and illuminates his path. He uses the spine of a human corpse as a whip, and his eyes dart around, capable of seeing far into the distance, even on the darkest nights. His appearance is terrifying, and his presence signals impending doom. He cannot speak, but when he stops riding, he calls out the name of the person who is about to die. Nothing can stop him once he sets out on his ride. The Dullahan is a solitary figure, rarely seen in groups, and his appearance is always a cause for great fear and dread. He is one of the most frightening figures in Irish mythology, a reminder of the inevitability of death and the power of the supernatural. He is often associated with Samhain, the Celtic festival that marks the end of summer and the beginning of winter, a time when the veil between the worlds of the living and the dead is thin.
subtitle: Headless harbinger of death
appearance: A headless rider on a black horse. He carries his own glowing head, often described as grotesque and grinning. He uses a human spine as a whip. His skin is often described as pale or decaying.
habitat: Rural areas, country roads, and graveyards, particularly at night.
behavior: The Dullahan rides silently, searching for his next victim. He is relentless and unstoppable. He is a solitary figure, rarely seen with others. He is drawn to places where death is near.

keyMyths.title: The Dullahan's Ride
keyMyths.description: The Dullahan rides through the countryside, his head held high, searching for the next person to die. When he stops, he calls out the name of his victim, marking them for death within the year. It is said that nothing can stop him once he has begun his ride.

sources.author: Lady Wilde
sources.work: Ancient Legends, Mystic Charms, and Superstitions of Ireland
sources.description: A collection of Irish folklore and legends, including stories of the Dullahan.
sources.text: A collection of Irish folklore and legends, including stories of the Dullahan.

sources.author: W.B. Yeats
